coredata.dc:title: Branched polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots as the high-performance friction-reducing and antiwear additives of polyethylene glycol
coredata.dc:creator: Mou, Zihao
Wang, Baogang
Lu, Hongsheng
Quan, Hongping
Huang, Zhiyu
coredata.dc:description: Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) grafted carbon dots (CDs-PEI0.6-10k) were facile synthesized by hydrothermal treatments of citric acid and PEI with various molecular weights of 0.6, 1.8 and 10 k. With the aid of protonation and anion exchange (between Cl− and bistrifluoromethanesulfonimide abbreviated as NTf2 -) processes in aqueous phase, the branched polyelectrolyte grafted CDs (CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2) derived from CDs-PEI0.6-10k were gained as the precipitates. The tribological properties of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as the lubricant additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were evaluated. The CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 exhibited superb friction-reducing and antiwear performances followed the sequence: CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 > CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 > CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2. Typically, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduced by 53.8% and 79.9%, respectively, when only 0.07 wt% of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 was introduced. The surface analysis results revealed that a tribochemical reaction film composed of iron compounds (including iron fluorides, sulfates, nitrides, oxides and carbides) and organic compounds containing various elements (such as C, O, N, S and F) should form on the rubbing surfaces lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion. The distinguished friction-reducing and antiwear properties of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 could be attributed to their nanolubrication effects and the above tribochemical film, i.e. the synergistic lubrication effect of their carbon cores and surface groups.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of friction-reducing and antiwear properties of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as additives of PEG200
Wear track surface analyses and lubrication mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedures of CDs-PEI0.6-10k and CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (a) CDs-PEI0.6k, (b) CDs-PEI1.8k and (c) CDs-PEI10k. Insets: particle size distributions of various CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns of (a) CDs-PEI0.6k, (b) CDs-PEI1.8k, (c) CDs-PEI10k, (d) CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2, (e) CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 and (e) CDs-PEI10k-NTf2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FTIR spectra of (a) CDs-PEI0.6k, (b) CDs-PEI1.8k, (c) CDs-PEI10k, (d) CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2, (e) CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 and (e) CDs-PEI10k-NTf2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Full-scan XPS spectra of various CDs and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) F1s and (f) S2p XPS spectra of CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TGA curves of (a) CDs-PEI0.6k, (b) CDs-PEI1.8k, (c) CDs-PEI10k, (d) CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2, (e) CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 and (e) CDs-PEI10k-NTf2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photos of various CDs/PEG200 dispersions after standing for one month at various temperatures and centrifuging at for 20 min 10000 rpm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Friction coefficient curves of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersions with various concentrations (c, wt%). (b) Mean friction coefficient and wear volume of lower disk lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersion as a function of c. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Mean friction coefficient and wear volume of lower disk lubricated by (a) PEG200, (b) CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 (0.7 wt%)/, (c) CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 (0.5 wt%)/and CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersions, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Mean friction coefficient and (b) wear volume of lower disk lubricated by pure PEG200, corresponding PEI/, LiNTf2/and CDs/PEG200 dispersions, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Microscopic image (a–d) and corresponding two-dimensional wear track profile (e–h) of lower disk lubricated by PEG200, CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 (0.7 wt%)/, CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 (0.5 wt%)/and CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersions, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FE-SEM images and corresponding elemental mappings of wear tracks lubricated by (a) PEG200 and (b) CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) High-resolution XPS Fe2p, C1s and O1s spectra of wear track of lower disk lubricated by PEG. (d–i) High-resolution XPS Fe2p, C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and F1s spectra of wear track of lower disk lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG dispersion. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Branched polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots as the high-performance friction-reducing and antiwear additives of polyethylene glycol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Chengdu, 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Chengdu, 610500, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) grafted carbon dots (CDs-PEI0.6-10k) were facile synthesized by hydrothermal treatments of citric acid and PEI with various molecular weights of 0.6, 1.8 and 10 k. With the aid of protonation and anion exchange (between Cl− and bistrifluoromethanesulfonimide abbreviated as NTf2 -) processes in aqueous phase, the branched polyelectrolyte grafted CDs (CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2) derived from CDs-PEI0.6-10k were gained as the precipitates. The tribological properties of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as the lubricant additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were evaluated. The CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 exhibited superb friction-reducing and antiwear performances followed the sequence: CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 > CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 > CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2. Typically, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduced by 53.8% and 79.9%, respectively, when only 0.07 wt% of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 was introduced. The surface analysis results revealed that a tribochemical reaction film composed of iron compounds (including iron fluorides, sulfates, nitrides, oxides and carbides) and organic compounds containing various elements (such as C, O, N, S and F) should form on the rubbing surfaces lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion. The distinguished friction-reducing and antiwear properties of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 could be attributed to their nanolubrication effects and the above tribochemical film, i.e. the synergistic lubrication effect of their carbon cores and surface groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Lubricant additives especially the friction-reducing and antiwear additives are the essence of high-performance lubricant oils. Polyethylene glycol (PEG), belonging to poly (alkyl ether)s, is a commonly used synthetic base oil for high temperature lubricants, gear oils, brake fluids, metal working fluids, compressor lubricants and fire-resistant hydraulic fluids owing to its many advantages over mineral oils, environmental friendly characteristics and degradability [1]. However, the conventional additives for mineral oils usually exhibit poor compatibility with PEG because it is a polar base oil. Hence, developing new and effective additive for PEG is of key importance for its practical applications in lubrication field.

Ionic liquids (ILs), especially imidazolium-based ILs, have become the most widely used additives of PEG because of their good compatibility with PEG and unique properties such as dipolar structure, low melting point, nonflammability, high thermal stability and negligible volatility [2–15]. Moreover, the IL modified nanoadditives, such as CuO nanorods, graphene, carbon dots (CDs), polyaniline nanoparticles, etc., were also applied as the efficient additives of PEG, which are hopeful to combine the advantages of both ILs and nanomaterials [16–20]. Considering the serious oxidation failure, corrosion and pollution problems of above IL-based additives, the CD-based additives are attracting more and more attention because of their small and uniform particle size, outstanding friction-reducing and antiwear performance, high thermal and chemical stability, and versatile chemical designability [21–23]. The chemical designability of CDs provides infinite possibilities for designing special CD-based additives and hence more efforts are urgently needed to be made on exploiting novel CD-based additives of PEG.

Since 2012, a lot of branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) grafted CDs (CDs-PEI) have been synthesized using citric acid, glucose, chitosan, etc. as the precursors by one-pot thermal treatments [24–32] or two-step methods [33–38]. The PEI groups endowed CDs-PEI with high fluorescence quantum yield, well water dispersibility and sensitive response to external stimulus. Therefore, the CDs-PEI have found wide applications in fluorescent sensing [25], cell imaging [33], gene delivery [35] and photocatalysis [39]. Unfortunately, the CDs-PEI have never been used as friction-reducing and antiwear additives of PEG yet despite the polymer grafted SiO2 nanoparticles have been reported as a particularly effective oil-based additives [40–42]. One of the largest obstacles for application of CDs-PEI in PEG lubrication is the poor dispersibility of CDs-PEI in PEG.

Herein, we synthesized the CDs-PEI0.6-10k by hydrothermal treatments of citric acid and PEI with different molecular weights (0.6 k, 1.8 k and 10 k). Just as described above, the CDs-PEI0.6-10k possessed good dispersion stability in water but could not be dispersed into PEG200 at all. Inspired by the good compatibility between the PEG and bistrifluoromethanesulfonimide (NTf2 -) [7], the CDs-PEI0.6-10k were transformed into polyelectrolyte grafted CDs (CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2) with the aid of protonation and subsequent anion exchange (between Cl− and NTf2 -) procedures in aqueous phase [43]. The CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 obtained as the precipitates exhibited excellent dispersion stability in PEG200 and hence their friction-reducing and antiwear properties as the additives of PEG200 were evaluated and compared by a UMT tribotester under steel/steel contact and ball-on-disk reciprocating mode. To probe the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2, the wear track surfaces lubricated by PEG200 containing CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 or not were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA, > 99.5%), PEI with different molecular weights of 0.6 k, 1.8 k and 10 k (>99%), LiNTf2 (>99.9%) and the PEG200 with an average molecular weight of 190–210 (AR) were purchased from the Sigma Aldrich. All the regents were used as received without further purification. HCl (AR, 36–38%) was provided by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of about 18.25 MΩ cm was prepared from a UPH-IV ultrapure water purifier (China) and used throughout the whole experimental processes.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the CDs-PEI0.6-10k were synthesized by a reported method with a few modifications [30]. Briefly, 2 g CA and 1 g PEI0.6k were mixed with 60 mL of ultrapure water to form a transparent solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stain-less steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 190 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the resultant solution was dialyzed against frequently changed water in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 5 days to remove the remaining molecular precursors. Finally, the water in the dialyzate was eliminated by freeze-drying and the CDs-PEI0.6k was obtained as a brown powder. Similarly, the CDs-PEI1.8k and CDs-PEI10k were also gained when the PEI0.6k was replaced by PEI1.8k and PEI10k, respectively. Based on the carbon source of CA, the yield of CDs-PEI0.6-10k is about 11.5, 12.5 and 13.0 wt%, respectively.

The CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 were prepared from CDs-PEI0.6-10k with the aid of protonation and subsequent anion exchange procedures in aqueous medium. Firstly, 0.1 g CDs-PEI0.6k were redispersed into 20 mL of 0.1 M HCl solution (pH value is about 1.00) under magnetic stirring for 1 h. The pH value of above CDs-PEI
0.6k
dispersion was measured as about 2.37. Considering that the pH of 0.1 M NH
4
Cl solution is about 5.13 at room temperature, the CDs-PEI0.6k was protonated and then 20 mL of 0.1 M LiNTf2 aqueous solution was added drop by drop into the above dispersion under fiercely magnetic stirring. During the dropping process, many red-brown precipitates were quickly formed and deposited out of the aqueous phase, meaning that the anion exchange process between Cl− and NTf2 - was adequate (conversion: > 98%) [44]. The precipitates were washed by 20 mL of ultrapure water for 6 times via filtering treatments and dried in an oven overnight at 40 °C.Then the CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 was obtained as a red-brown powder. Similarly, the CDs-PEI1.8k-NTf2 and CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 were also prepared according to above procedures.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a TEM (FEI Tecnai G20) system operating at 200 kV. The specimens were prepared by drop-casting CDs aqueous dispersions (0.02 mg/mL) onto the carbon-coated copper grids, followed by drying at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out using a diffractometer (Bruker D8 advance X Pert Pro) with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15418 nm at room temperature. The interlayer distance was calculated in terms of Bragg's equation:
2 d sin θ = nλ
where d, θ, n and λ is the interlayer distance, peak position, series of diffraction (n = 1) and incident wavelength (0.15418 nm), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were gained on a spectrophotometer (WQF-520) by the KBr pellet method with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (STA449F3) under the Ar atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were gained on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV).

The tribological properties of PEG200 containing additives or not used in steel/steel contacts were performed on a Bruker Universal Mechanical Tester (UMT) platform with a ball-on-disk configuration. Specifically, the friction and wear test was carried out by pressing an upper running AISI52100 steel ball (12.7 mm in diameter, hardness of about 59–61 HRC) over a lower stationary AISI52100 steel disk under reciprocating sliding mode (linear velocity, 10 mm/s; amplitude, 5 mm; duration, 20 min; load, 40 N; and room temperature). The wear track widths and depths of lower disks were measured using a three-dimensional optical microscope, and friction coefficients were recorded automatically by a data-acquiring system linked to the UMT tribotester. In addition, the wear volumes of lower disks were also roughly calculated by integrating the sectional areas of the two-dimensional wear track profiles. The friction coefficient was calculated as an average value of all the data points on the friction coefficient curve. Three replicate measurements were made for each friction process to get the convincing mean friction coefficient and wear volume values with error bars.

After friction tests, the wear track surfaces of lower disks were cleaned thoroughly by ultrasonic treatment in absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 min. Then the morphologies and elemental compositions of the wear track surfaces were analyzed by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (JSM-7500F, Japan) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS). In addition, the XPS data were also gained to reveal the possible tribochemical reactions occurred on the wear track surfaces.

TEM images shown in Fig. 2 confirm the formation of nearly monodisperse CDs-PEI0.6-10k with the spherical morphology. Some representative CDs are marked out by white circles. The average particle size of CDs-PEI0.6k, CDs-PEI1.8k and CDs-PEI10k is about 4.9, 5.2 and 5.3 nm by counting more than 50 particles, respectively. The similar particle size implies that the effect of molecular weight of PEI on particle size of CDs-PEI is negligible. Moreover, the lattice structures of graphite can hardly be found in TEM images, revealing that the CDs-PEI0.6-10k are almost amorphous.

The XRD patterns of CDs-PEI0.6-10k (Fig. 3 a–c) all exhibit a short and broad peak at 21.6° corresponding to the interlayer distance of about 0.41 nm. The obviously enlarged interlayer distance compared with graphitic structure (0.34 nm) can be owing to the highly disordered carbon atoms (i.e. amorphous structures) and surface PEI groups of CDs-PEI0.6-10k [45], which is well consistent with the results of TEM images. The XRD diffraction peak at 21.6° obviously shifts to 17.6° (Fig. 3d–f) when the CDs-PEI0.6-10k were transformed into the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2, meaning that the interlayer distance is further enlarged due to the successful attachment of relatively big anions (NTf2 -) onto the surface PEI groups.

The infrared characteristic peaks of PEI at 2939 and 1557 cm−1, assigned to the C-H stretching vibrations of methylene and N-H deformation vibrations of amino, appear in the FTIR spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10k (Fig. 4 a–c). In addition, the peaks at 3433, 1700 and 1656 cm−1, belonging to the O-H/N-H stretching vibrations, C=O stretching vibrations of the amide groups and C=C stretching vibrations, can also be found, suggesting the PEI groups are covalently grafted onto the surfaces of CDs-PEI0.6-10k by amide groups. In the spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 (Fig. 4d–f), three strong peaks at 1343, 1194 and 1052 cm−1 attributed to C-F and S=O stretching vibrations of NTf2 - are observed, directly proving that the NTf2 - anions are decorated onto the surfaces of CDs-PEI0.6-10k.

Full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 5 a) reflect that the CDs-PEI0.6-10k are composed of C, O and N elements while the CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 are composed of C, O, N, F and S elements, further confirming that the polyelectrolyte grafted CDs were successfully prepared and the process of anion exchange between Cl− and NTf2 - is sufficient because no Cl element was detected. The chemical compositions shown in Table S1 demonstrate a similar composition for the CDs-PEI0.6-10k and CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2, respectively, indicating that the synthesis methods for various CDs are reliable. The high-resolution elemental XPS spectra of CDs-PEI0.6k (Fig. S1) and CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 (Fig. 5b–f) are fitted into different peaks and compared. The C1s XPS spectrum of CDs-PEI0.6k (Fig. S1a) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.1, 285.2 and 287.3 eV attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O and C=O, respectively [46]. Two new C species of C-S (288.5 eV) and C-F (292.6 eV) stemmed from NTf2 - are found in C1s XPS spectrum of CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 (Fig. 5b). The three peaks in Fig. S1b at 398.6, 399.4 and 400.1 eV are ascribed to the N-H (-NH2), N-H (-NH-) and C-N. Except that in Figs. S1b and a new peak at 402.3 eV (S-N-S) in Fig. 5c is also observed. Besides the O species of C=O (530.2 eV) and C-O (531.4 eV) in Figs. S1c and a new O specie of S=O (532.7 eV) appears in Fig. 5d. Moreover, the corresponding F and S species are also observed in F1s and S2p XPS spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 (Fig. 5e and f). The XPS results verify that the surfaces of CDs-PEI0.6-10k and CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 are grafted by certain amounts of polymer and polyelectrolyte groups, respectively.

TGA analyses shown in Fig. 6 indicate that all of CDs-PEI0.6-10k and CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 possess high thermal stability. Despite the thermal stability of CDs-PEI0.6-10k seems to slightly decrease with increasing molecular weight of PEI, the decomposition temperature of CDs-PEI10k is still high (about 312 °C). The decomposition temperature of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 is further enhanced to about 346 °C. The weight loss for all of CDs can be divided into two stages with increasing temperature. When the temperature is lower than 200 °C, the weight losses for various CDs are moderate and in the range of 2.66–9.60 wt%, which can be attributed to the volatilization of moisture. It can be found that the moisture of hydrophilic CDs-PEI0.6-10k is obviously higher than that of hydrophobic CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2. Once the temperature is higher than 200 °C, the weight losses of various CDs are sharp and mainly due to the thermal decomposition of their surface groups, the weight fractions (wt%) of PEI0.6-10k in CDs-PEI0.6-10k are estimated to be 41.04, 46.18 and 46.65 wt% (marked out by black dash line), respectively. Similarly, the weight fractions of PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 in the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 are about 58.17, 60.73 and 62.72 wt% (marked out by red dash line), respectively. Accordingly, about 17.13, 14.55 and 16.07 wt% of NTf2 - are attached on the surfaces of CDs-PEI0.6-10k during the anion exchange process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of friction-reducing and antiwear properties of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as additives of PEG200
ce:section.ce:para: As expected, the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 could be well dispersed into the PEG200 to form transparent solution by ultrasonic treatments, which is of key importance for their application as additives. The stabilities of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersions with optimal concentrations in term of their tribological properties were evaluated by standing for a long time at various temperatures and centrifuging at high speed. As shown in Fig. 7 , all the dispersions display excellent stabilities not only under common condition (standing for one month at 25 °C), but also under harsh conditions (standing for one month at −20 °C and 150 °C and centrifuging for 20 min at 10000 rpm). The super compatibility between CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 and PEG200 is further confirmed by the photos of representative CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 (0.05 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion under sunlight and UV irradiation (365 nm). Fig. S2b shows that the dispersion under 365 nm irradiation emits bright blue-white fluorescence, indicating that the CDs-PEI0.6k-NTf2 can realize monodisperse state in PEG200. These results suggest that the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as additives of PEG200 are hopeful to find promising application under harsh conditions.

During the friction tests under specific conditions, the friction coefficient curves, using PEG200 containing CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 or not as lubricants, were recorded and shown in the Fig. S3a, S4a and Fig. 8 a, respectively. The friction coefficient curves of all the dispersions are significantly lower and smoother than PEG200, reflecting the outstanding friction-reducing ability of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2. It is worth noting that all of additives possess an optimal c. The optimal c for CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 is 0.7, 0.5 and 0.07 wt%, respectively. After the friction tests, the mean friction coefficients and mean wear volumes, lubricated by the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersions with various c, were calculated based on the friction coefficient curves and wear tracks of lower disks and shown in the Fig. S3b, S4b and Fig. 8b, respectively. The mean friction coefficient and wear volume both illustrate a first decreased then increased tendency with increasing c. Hence, the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 demonstrate the best friction-reducing and antiwear performances at c of 0.7, 0.5 and 0.07 wt%, respectively, which is well consistent with the results of friction coefficient curves.

The performances of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 were compared at their respective optimal c. As shown in Fig. 9 , using CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 as additives, the largest friction coefficient and wear volume reductions for PEG200 are 41.3 and 75.8%, 43.6 and 78.1%, and 53.8 and 79.9%, respectively. Obviously, the CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 is the best additive because it endows the largest friction and wear reductions with PEG200 at the particularly low c (0.07 wt%).

To further reveal the distinguished performance of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2, the PEI0.6-10k and LiNTf2 were also used as the additives and compared with the CDs-based additives under the same c. As shown in Fig. 10 , using PEI0.6-10k or LiNTf2 as the additives, the mean friction coefficient and wear volume reductions for PEG200 are in the ranges of 6.1–12.6% and 14.2–24.0%, respectively, which are much lower than that (41.3–53.8% and 75.8–79.9%) of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2. These results verify that the carbon cores of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 should play an extremely important role in reducing friction and wear. To sum up, the outstanding friction-reducing and antiwear performance of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 are considered to be attributed to the synergistic effect of their surface groups and carbon cores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear track surface analyses and lubrication mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 gives microscopic images and corresponding two-dimensional wear track profiles of lower disks lubricated by PEG200 and CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersions at the optimal c. The wear track lubricated by PEG200 (Fig. 11a and e) are deep and wide with a big depth of 5.33 μm and width of 540.3 μm owing to the poor lubrication ability of base oil. The depth and width of wear track lubricated by the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2/PEG200 dispersions (Fig. 11b–d and f-h) largely reduce to 2.83 and 365.5 μm, 2.52 and 343.0 μm and 2.37 and 318.6 μm, respectively, directly reflecting the extremely good antiwear performance of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2. Thereinto, the CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 exhibits the best antiwear ability at the lowest c of 0.07 wt%.

To probe the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2, the FE-SEM coupled with EDS were used to analyze the wear tracks lubricated by PEG200 and CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion. As shown in Fig. 12 , a lot of scratches can be found in both of wear tracks lubricated by PEG200 and dispersion. Moreover, some abrasive dusts also appear in wear track lubricated by PEG200, meaning that the surface suffered from severe wear. The elemental mappings demonstrate that the Fe, Cr, C and O elements uniformly distribute on the wear track lubricated by PEG200. For wear track lubricated by dispersion, the homogeneous N, S and F element distributions are also found, implying that the CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 must participate in the formation of boundary lubrication film. The elemental compositions in Table S2 further reveal that the CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 especially their surface groups play an important role in the formation of boundary lubrication film.

To further gain insight into the friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2, the high-resolution elemental XPS spectra were obtained to disclose the chemical states of typical elements on wear track surfaces lubricated by PEG200 and CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 (0.07 wt%)/PEG200 dispersion. Fig. 13 a–c illustrate that the iron oxides (such as Fe2O3 at 726.4 eV and FeO at 710.3 eV) and C-O compounds are formed on the surface of wear track lubricated by PEG200 [9,20]. On the surface of wear track lubricated by dispersion, besides iron oxides, the iron fluorides (718.4 eV) and carbides (708.1 eV) can also be found in Fig. 13d. Meanwhile, Fig. 13e–i displays the compounds composed of C, O, N, S and F elements are formed. In addition, the iron nitrides (396.2 eV) and iron sulfates (164.7 eV) are found in Fig. 13g and h [7]. The XPS data demonstrate that the tribochemical reactions between metal surface and surface groups of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 should take place during the friction process.

Based on the above results of property evaluation and surface analyses, we tried to discuss the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 as additives. During the friction process, the steel/steel contact surface will become positively charged because the low-energy electrons are prone to emit from contact points [5]. The CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 quickly absorb and embed into the rubbing surfaces by electrostatic interactions. Under the collective effect of high pressure and frictional heat on the rubbing surfaces, some complex tribochemical reactions between metal surface and surface groups of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 should occur. Then the tribochemical reaction film, composed of iron compounds (including iron oxides, carbides, nitrides, sulfates and fluorides) and organic compounds containing various elements (such as C, O, N, S and F), form on the rubbing surfaces. Meanwhile, as the wear track surface lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 dispersion is much smoother and cleaner than that lubricated by PEG200 (Fig. 12) [20,47–50], the carbon cores of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 should also play the effective rolling, mending and polishing effects during the friction process. To sum up, the nanolubrication effects and the formed tribochemical film can corporately account for the excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2.

The CDs-PEI0.6-10k were synthesized by hydrothermal treatments of CA and PEI0.6-10k. Then the CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 were prepared from CDs-PEI0.6-10k with the aid of protonation and subsequent anion exchange processes. The CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 exhibited superb dispersion stability in PEG200 and excellent friction-reducing and antiwear performance as additives. The CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 showed the best performance at a particularly low optimal c. Specifically, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 reduced by 53.8 and 79.9%, respectively, when 0.07 wt% of CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 was introduced. The wear track surface analyses demonstrated that the a tribochemical reaction film composed of iron compounds (including iron oxides, carbides, nitrides, sulfates and fluorides) and organic compounds containing various elements (such as C, O, N, S and F) was formed on the rubbing surface lubricated by CDs-PEI10k-NTf2 dispersion. Thus, the friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of CDs-PEI0.6-10k-NTf2 could be well explained by their nanolubrication effects and the tribochemical films, i.e. the synergistic lubrication effect of their surface groups and carbon cores.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of the Opening Project of Oil & Gas Field Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (YQKF201402), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project of China (2015M582567) and the Postdoctoral Special Fund for Research of Sichuan Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.04.066.
